coredata.dc:title: Carbon shell-coated mackinawite FeS platelets as anode materials for high-performance sodium-ion batteries

coredata.dc:description: Mackinawite iron sulfide (M-FeS), a rarely reported conversion-type anode material, undergoes repeated volume changes during the charge–discharge process, which eventually induces fast electrochemical degradation. However, commonly used strategies to mitigate volume changes using hybridization with carbon materials require high temperatures above 500 °C, which is limited for M-FeS due to its low thermal stability. Here, a novel and facile strategy to hybridize M-FeS with a carbon material using a one-step hydrothermal method under low-temperature conditions (125 °C) is reported. Carbon dots with functional groups were served as nucleation sites, and hybridized with M-FeS. The hybrid material (M-FeS@C) comprised micron-sized M-FeS particles wrapped with a carbon shell and exhibited improved structural stability during the charge–discharge process when tested for sodium-ion storage; stable cycle performance was achieved for 500 cycles delivering a capacity of 372 mAh g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. Moreover, when Ox-SWCNTs were also used as a conductive agent, a three-dimensional (3D) conductive network that provides electrical pathways was formed and preserved. The synergic effects of the carbon shell and CNT conductive agent (M-FeS@C+CNT) maximized the structural stability and the electrical conduction path, resulting in a capacity of 336 mAh g−1 at a high current density of 10 A g−1 and a capacity of 360 mAh g−1 for 860 cycles at 1 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Carbon shell-coated mackinawite FeS platelets as anode materials for high-performance sodium-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mackinawite iron sulfide (M-FeS), a rarely reported conversion-type anode material, undergoes repeated volume changes during the charge–discharge process, which eventually induces fast electrochemical degradation. However, commonly used strategies to mitigate volume changes using hybridization with carbon materials require high temperatures above 500 °C, which is limited for M-FeS due to its low thermal stability. Here, a novel and facile strategy to hybridize M-FeS with a carbon material using a one-step hydrothermal method under low-temperature conditions (125 °C) is reported. Carbon dots with functional groups were served as nucleation sites, and hybridized with M-FeS. The hybrid material (M-FeS@C) comprised micron-sized M-FeS particles wrapped with a carbon shell and exhibited improved structural stability during the charge–discharge process when tested for sodium-ion storage; stable cycle performance was achieved for 500 cycles delivering a capacity of 372 mAh g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. Moreover, when Ox-SWCNTs were also used as a conductive agent, a three-dimensional (3D) conductive network that provides electrical pathways was formed and preserved. The synergic effects of the carbon shell and CNT conductive agent (M-FeS@C+CNT) maximized the structural stability and the electrical conduction path, resulting in a capacity of 336 mAh g−1 at a high current density of 10 A g−1 and a capacity of 360 mAh g−1 for 860 cycles at 1 A g−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of the abundance, inexpensiveness, and chemical properties similar to those of lithium, sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have been considered a promising alternative to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) [1,2]. However, the large size and mass of sodium ions lead to sluggish electrochemical properties [3,4], which restrict the practical applications of SIBs. To overcome this limitation, appropriate electrode materials should be chosen as host materials for sodium-ion storage [5–9].

Iron sulfide Fe x S y , a natural ore that is abundant and inexpensive, has been studied as a potential anode material for secondary-ion batteries such as lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) because of its high theoretical capacity, which is based on a conversion reaction with alkali-metal-ions [10–13]. Depending on the ratio between x and y, each Fe x S y iron sulfide phase has different physical and chemical characteristics and thus displays varied electrochemical properties [14–19]. Among various phases, only mackinawite FeS (M-FeS) (Fe1+
x S, 0 < x < 0.11) has high electrical conductivity (∼100 S cm−1) and easily accessible two-dimensional (2D) ion-transport channels [20]. Considering these advantages, M-FeS is considered as a promising anode material for SIBs.

During the charge–discharge process, iron sulfide phases undergo a reversible conversion reaction through several steps, which involve large and repeated volume changes [21–23]. These processes cause pulverization and loss of active material, eventually leading to the fast capacity decay and unstable cycle performance [24–30], as also observed in lithium-ion storage properties of M-FeS [31,32].

To address the above-mentioned issues, the most commonly used strategy to accommodate a volume change of conversion- or alloying-type active materials is to construct a hybrid structure with carbon materials, resulting in a yolk–shell structure, carbon coating, etc. [33–43], which effectively alleviates a volume change of active materials [26,44–53]. However, these methods require harsh conditions such as high temperatures (above 500 °C) for carbonization, and entail several complicated procedures.

M-FeS has low thermal stability; it undergoes phase transition to thermally stable iron sulfide at a relatively low temperature (∼200 °C) [54,55]. Therefore, for M-FeS, the construction of hybrid structure with carbon materials using a high-temperature process is limited. For this reason, although M-FeS has several advantages as anode materials for SIBs, it has not been evaluated as anode materials for SIBs. Therefore, an unusual strategy to fabricate a hybrid structure with carbon materials under mild conditions with a facile process is required.

Here, we report a novel strategy to construct a carbon shell on the surface of M-FeS. A carbon shell was formed on micron-sized M-FeS (M-FeS@C) using a one-step hydrothermal process under low-temperature (∼125 °C) in the presence of carbon dots with functional groups which serve as nucleation sites of M-FeS. The carbon shell increased the structural stability and helped maintain the original particle structure of the M-FeS, resulting in stable cycle performance during the charge–discharge process. In addition, a three-dimensional (3D) conductive network was achieved throughout the electrode by replacing carbon black (Super P), a conventional conductive agent, with Ox-SWCNTs (M-FeS@C+CNT). The synergic effect of the carbon shell and Ox-SWCNTs conductive agent led to a remarkable improvement in the rate capability and cycle performance; a capacity of 336 mAh g−1 at a high current density of 10 A g−1 and 366 mAh g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 for 860 cycles were achieved. The novel hybridization strategy combined with a 3D conductive network construction provides an efficient method to improve the stability of conversion- or alloying-type battery electrodes during the charge–discharge process.

Pristine SWCNTs were highly oxidized using previously reported procedures [56]. For example, 5 g of the SWCNT powder (75 % purity, purchased from OCSiAl) was ground with 56 g of NaClO3 by mixer and then mixed with 50 mL of the fuming nitric acid under kneading to promote the oxidation reaction at room temperature. After 1 h resting, the SWCNT/NaClO3/fuming nitric acid dough was diluted with massive water and purified by centrifugal method to remove residual acid and oxidant molecules. The prepared oxidized SWCNTs (Ox-SWCNTs) up to 10 μm in length were redispersed in DI water by using a horn-sonicator (power 500 W) for 30 min, which can give enough energy to generate the length distribution of the Ox-SWCNTs by destroying highly oxidized CNT structures. After sonication, dispersed Ox-SWCNTs were centrifuged three times at 13000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant solution was taken.

M-FeS@C was synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. First, CNT dispersion was transferred to a 100-ml Teflon-lined autoclave. Then 0.1 mol of sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O) was put into CNT dispersion with vigorous stirring for 10 min, followed by adding 0.1 mol of iron (Fe powder). The autoclave was purged with argon gas for 10 min, then tightly sealed and retained at 125 °C for 6 d. The resultant slurry was washed and filtered three times with deionized water, then freeze-dried for 3 d. To obtain different concentrations of M-FeS@C, the mass of Ox-SWCNTs was adjusted based on the mass of 0.1 mol of Fe and 0.1 mol of Na2S·9H2O during synthesis. Samples are denoted as M-FeS@Cm, where m indicates the wt% of Ox-SWCNTs. Pristine M-FeS was prepared under the same synthesis procedure as that of M-FeS@C except without the Ox-SWCNTs dispersion.

The length of Ox-SWCNTs was observed using dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a Rigaku D/MAX-2500 with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). Scanning electron microscopy/energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM/EDS) was conducted using a Hitachi S-4800. A Focused ion beam (FIB) with a Ga ion source was performed using FEI Helios 600. Transmission electron microscopy/electron energy loss spectroscopy (TEM/EELS) images were taken on a JOEL JEM-2200FS using a specimen prepared by Focused ion beam (FIB). CNT morphologies were characterized using atomic force microscopy (AFM, Digital Instruments Multimode). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi. Raman spectra were taken using Raman spectroscopy (Alpha300R, WITec) using a laser with a 633-nm wavelength as the excitation source. A Q50 thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, TA instrument) was used to calculate the weight percent of carbon content. An ASAP 2020 Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, Micromeritics) measurement was conducted to measure specific surface area. Four-point probe measurements were conducted to measure the sheet resistance of the pelletized powder samples with a diameter of 13 mm.

Active material, conductive agent (Timcal C65 super P carbon black or Ox-SWCNTs), sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, MTI Korea), and SBR binder (MTI Korea) were fully mixed in a weight ratio of 85:5:5:5. Note that for the M-FeS@C+CNT electrode, Ox-SWCNTs were used as a conductive agent. The homogeneous slurry was cast on Cu foil by using a doctor blade, then dried at 80 °C for 1 h, followed by drying at 100 °C 12 h in a vacuum oven. The mass loading of the electrode (diameter, 14 mm) was 1 ∼ 1.5 mg cm−2, and specific capacity was calculated based on the total mass of the electrode. The coin cells were assembled in an Ar-filled glove box. The battery assembly was performed in a half-cell configuration. A coin cell (CR2032) with Na metal as the counter and reference electrodes was used to study the electrochemical characteristics of the samples. The electrolyte of 1 M NaPF6 in diglyme and GF/F membrane were used as electrolyte and separator, respectively. The electrochemical test was carried out on a WBCS3000 electrochemical workstation (Wonatech, South Korea). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was carried out on Ivium equipment and software in the frequency range from 1 MHz to 0.1 Hz with an amplitude of 10 mV. The electrode after cycling was disassembled and washed with diglyme for ex situ analysis.

Fig. 1 a and b illustrate the structure of M-FeS and the Ox-SWCNTs, respectively. For hydrothermal synthesis, we adopted SWCNTs with numerous functional groups on the outer surfaces to facilitate the dispersion of the SWCNTs in the water without using dispersant molecules [56]. Moreover, precursor ions (Fe2+ and S2−) can interact with functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups [57–60]. Notably, under acoustic cavitation with horn sonication for an extended period (e.g., longer than 1 h), the Ox-SWCNTs structure can be destroyed to form tiny carbon dots [61–63], which can positively influence the nucleation and growth process of M-FeS in aqueous solution to form hierarchical structures [64–66]. The synthesis conditions (125 °C) for M-FeS@C are similar to those for M-FeS, except that the water was replaced with a water-based dispersion of CNTs [20]. Before the one-step hydrothermal synthesis, we intentionally controlled the length of the Ox-SWCNTs with the aid of strong acoustic cavitation energy generated by horn-type sonication. After 30 min of sonication, the Ox-SWCNTs with an initial length as large as 10 μm were transformed into carbon dots with dimensions of 20–40 nm and Ox-SWCNTs chains with lengths as large as a few hundred nanometers, as observed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. S1a); these small sizes were confirmed using atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. S1b). The detailed morphology of the Ox-SWCNTs after 30 min of sonication is shown in Fig. S2. In contrast to the pristine Ox-SWCNTs that have a little crystalline structure (Fig. S2a), carbon dots with no observable crystalline structure were formed (Fig. S2b), which is attributed to the collapse of the Ox-SWCNTs’ structure during the oxidation reaction and additional destructive sonication process [56,67]. The carbon dots’ sizes determined by DLS and AFM are similar, and these results affirm that carbon dots originated from Ox-SWCNTs during sonication. The whole synthesis process of the M-FeS@C sample is illustrated in Fig. 1c.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns show that well-developed (001) orientation (2θ = 17.7°) vertical to the c-axis was dominant for the M-FeS and M-FeS@C samples (Fig. 2 a). This result indicates successful synthesis of the 2D crystal structure of mackinawite (JCPDS 15–0037) with no impurities [68–70]. Although the introduction of Ox-SWCNTs at concentrations up to 1.5 wt% led to a decrease in intensity of the (001) peak, the formation of the original crystal structure was not disturbed; the XRD patterns displayed the same peak positions. However, when the concentration of Ox-SWCNTs increased to greater than 1.5 wt%, the (001) peak intensity decreased sharply; when the concentration of Ox-SWCNTs was 2.2 wt%, Fe peak appeared at 2θ ≈ 44°, and finally, only the Fe peak was observed when the Ox-SWCNTs concentration was 4.4 wt% (Fig. S3). These results indicate that a high concentration of Ox-SWCNTs restricts the nucleation and growth of M-FeS. The effect of the Ox-SWCNTs’ introduction on the nucleation and growth of M-FeS was confirmed by the change in crystallite size of the M-FeS, as calculated using the Scherrer equation [71]. This change indicates that the interaction between precursor ions (Fe2+ and S2−) and Ox-SWCNTs affected the energy required for nucleation and growth during hydrothermal synthesis (Fig. S4).

The effect of the introduced Ox-SWCNTs was clearly observed in field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images (Fig. 2b). The M-FeS@C samples exhibited a plate-type bulk morphology with sizes of 2–5 μm and a Fe/S atomic ratio of approximately 1, which is similar to that of pristine M-FeS (Fig. 2c). However, the concentration of Ox-SWCNTs affected the surface morphologies of the M-FeS@C samples. When the concentration of Ox-SWCNTs was low (0.44 wt%), the M-FeS@C exhibited a rough surface composed of small particles with sizes of several tens of nanometers, and these particles fully covered the M-FeS particles (Fig. S5). When the concentration of Ox-SWCNTs was increased (to 1 or 1.5 wt%), the size of these small particles decreased and their areal density increased. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption measurements indicate that M-FeS, M-FeS@C0.44, and M-FeS@C1.0 exhibit very small Brunauer−Emmett−Teller (BET) specific surface areas around 1.0 ∼ 2.3 m2 g−1, which implies that the addition of carbon dots does not affect to make the porous structure (Fig. S6). However, the plate-type morphology was not observed when the Ox-SWCNTs concentration was greater than 1.5 wt%. At 2.2 wt% Ox-SWCNTs, M-FeS with an irregular shape and aggregated Ox-SWCNTs were intertwined, and at 4.4 wt% Ox-SWCNTs, spherical Fe particles with some plate-type M-FeS on their surface were also intertwined with aggregated Ox-SWCNTs (Fig. S7).

The sonication conditions used to disperse the Ox-SWCNTs also affected their surface morphology (Fig. S8). After a short sonication time (5 min), the particles on the surface of the M-FeS@C were larger, and the surface coverage was less uniform, resulting in a rougher surface than that observed after a long sonication time (60 min). Given that a short sonication time is inevitable for dispersing the Ox-SWCNTs and that the length of the Ox-SWCNTs decreases with increasing sonication time, these changes in surface morphology indicate that the length of the Ox-SWCNTs, especially the carbon dots, are involved in the formation of the M-FeS@C surface.

Detailed morphology of M-FeS@C was observed using cross-sectional transmission electron microscopy (TEM) in conjunction with focused ion beam (FIB) specimen preparation (Fig. 3 ). The images show that M-FeS@C1.0 had a wavy surface region composed of an amorphous matrix with no lattice, along with crystalline spherical nanoparticles (white arrow in Fig. 3b) with a diameter of 4–5 nm evenly embedded in the amorphous matrix (Fig. 3a, b); the lattice distance observed in the spherical nanoparticles was 0.25 nm, which corresponds to the (110) facet of M-FeS (red circle and white arrow in Fig. 3b and Fig. S9) [72]. In the bulk region below the surface, the (001) and (110) orientations of M-FeS were observed and no structures related to Ox-SWCNTs were found (Fig. S10). The distributions of elements in M-FeS@C1.0 were observed using electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) maps (Fig. 3c). EELS maps of the corresponding image clearly show that the surface region of M-FeS@C1.0 consists of a dense carbon and oxygen layer with a thickness of 20–30 nm and dispersed iron and sulfur (M-FeS nanoparticles) inside the carbon and oxygen layer, whereas iron and sulfur are predominantly observed in the bulk region. This configuration indicates that the bulk M-FeS is covered with an oxygen-containing surface carbon matrix in which M-FeS nanoparticles are embedded. The M-FeS@C0.44 sample exhibited the same structure observed for M-FeS@C1.0 except but with some variation in the thickness of carbon matrix; thinner carbon matrix region (under 20 nm) and thicker carbon matrix (∼60 nm) compared to that of M-FeS@C1.0 (Fig. S11). Given the surface morphology observed in the SEM images, these results indicate that the thickness of the carbon matrix depends on the concentration of the Ox-SWCNTs.

To confirm the origin of the amorphous oxygen-containing carbon matrix, we conducted XPS analysis (Fig. 3d). Functional groups of pristine Ox-SWCNTs were observed on the surface of M-FeS@C1.0 [56], indicating that the carbon on the surface originated from the Ox-SWCNTs. The size and shape of the convex part in the wavy structure on the surface observed by TEM well match the size of carbon dots characterized using DLS, AFM, and SEM images. Therefore, we infer that the carbon dots induce nucleation and growth of M-FeS nanoparticles and are located on the surface of M-FeS@C during the hydrothermal synthesis process to form the carbon shell.

Unlike the surface region, the carbon component was not observed in the bulk region by TEM observation because of the few-micron thickness of the M-FeS@C particle in the cross-sectional direction. Therefore, Raman spectroscopy analysis was conducted to confirm the existence of carbon in the bulk region after etching the surface region (Fig. 3e). The Raman spectrum of M-FeS@C1.0 shows vibrational bands of M-FeS (223, 284, and 399 cm−1) and α-Fe2O3 (1299 cm−1), which indicate slight surface oxidation [70]. The D and G peaks of the carbon were very weak, indicating that the carbon dots on the surface of the M-FeS@C exhibit a defective and amorphous state [73,74]. However, when the surface region of the M-FeS@C was etched using reactive ion etching (RIE), a sharp G peak at ∼ 1600 cm−1 and a weak D peak at ∼ 1314 cm−1 originating from the pristine Ox-SWCNTs were observed [56]. These results show that carbon dots that covered the surface of the M-FeS were amorphous states with no lattice, as observed in the corresponding TEM images (Fig. 3b, Fig. S11c). The existence of CNTs with a crystalline state in the bulk region of M-FeS is probably because some CNTs were less destroyed, unlike the carbon dots. To confirm the amorphous state of the carbon dots, a CNT dispersion after sonication (30 min) was drop-coated onto a silicon wafer and subsequently characterized by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S12). Both graphitic (I D/I G ≈ 0.37) and amorphous (I D/I G ≈ 1.12) regions appeared in the coating region simultaneously, implying the coexistence of the amorphous and crystalline states, as observed in Fig. 3e. A lower sheet resistance for M-FeS@C1.0 compared with that of pristine M-FeS confirms that CNTs dispersed in the bulk region contribute to electron conduction (Table S1). We propose that the as-synthesized M-FeS@C exhibits a hybrid structure (Fig. 3f); embedded crystalline CNT shreds inside the M-FeS and a carbon shell outside the M-FeS, resulting in a conductive network connecting the inside and outside of the particles. The mechanism of formation of the M-FeS@C hybrid structure is illustrated in Fig. S13.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of M-FeS@C and M-FeS@C+CNT anodes
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of the carbon shell on the electrochemical performance of M-FeS, coin-type half-cell tests were conducted in the potential range of 0.01 ∼ 3.0 V (vs Na/Na+). The M-FeS@C samples exhibited different electrochemical properties depending on the concentration of Ox-SWCNTs (Fig. S14). M-FeS@C1.0 exhibited better cycle performance than M-FeS@C0.44 and M-FeS@C1.5, which is attributed to the optimized thickness of its carbon shell (Fig. 3a, Fig. S11a) and to its low sheet resistance (Table S1) [75–77]. The sonication time of Ox-SWCNTs in the synthesis process of M-FeS@C also affected their electrochemical properties (Fig. S15). For M-FeS@C1.0, although M-FeS@C1.0 (5 min sonication) displayed inferior rate capability compared with M-FeS@C1.0 (30 min sonication), the rate capability of M-FeS@C1.0 (60 min sonication) was similar to that of M-FeS@C1.0 (30 min sonication) (Fig. 4 a, Fig. S15a). This result is attributable to the optimized surface morphology, which indicates that sufficient sonication time (i.e., CNT length control) is essential to construct a uniform carbon shell [78,79]. Therefore, we selected M-FeS@C1.0 (30 min sonication) for comparison with the electrochemical properties of M-FeS based on its rate capability and cycle performance (Fig. 4).

The rate capabilities of electrodes were tested at various current densities ranging from 0.05 to 10 A g−1 (Fig. 4a). Compared with the rate capability of M-FeS, that of M-FeS@C was similar at low current densities but much improved at high current densities. The M-FeS@C achieved reversible specific capacities of 463, 447, 434, 427, 418, 400, 364, 332, 279, and 181 mAh g−1 at current densities of 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, and 10 A g−1, respectively. When the current density was recovered to 0.2 A g−1 from the high-rate of 10 A g−1, both electrodes recovered their initial capacity. The improved rate capability of the M-FeS@C electrode compared with that of the M-FeS electrode is attributed to the reduced sheet resistance due to the carbon shell on the surface and to the embedded CNT shreds in the bulk region.

In addition to the effect of the carbon shell, we replaced carbon black (Super P), which is commonly used as a conductive agent, with pristine Ox-SWCNTs to construct a 3D conductive network and preserve the electrical conduction pathways; the resultant composite is denoted as M-FeS@C+CNT. Surprisingly, the rate capabilities greatly improved when the CNTs were used as the conductive agent. The M-FeS@C+CNT achieved reversible specific capacities of 505, 486, 471, 464, 454, 438, 418, 403, 380, and 332 mAh g−1 at current densities of 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, and 10 A g−1, respectively. The capacity differences among all the samples were maximized at a high current density of 10 A g−1. Free-standing CNT electrode contribute little to the capacity at current densities above 0.3 A g−1 (Fig. S16), and only 5 wt% of the CNT conductive agent was added when fabricating the electrodes. Thus, the aforementioned results suggest that the Ox-SWCNTs result in more effective electrical paths than the conventional carbon black (Super P) conductive agent during charge–discharge processes rather than contributing to the capacity [57,80–83]. The charge–discharge profiles with various current densities clearly indicate that the M-FeS@C+CNT electrode exhibits superior rate capability (Fig. 4b, Fig. S17c, d). The galvanostatic voltage profiles of the M-FeS@C+CNT for the first five cycles at a current density of 0.05 A g−1 indicate that the discharge and charge capacity in the first cycle was 584 and 505 mAh g−1, respectively, and that the coulomb efficiency was 86.5 % (Fig. 4c). The irreversible capacity loss, which was also observed for M-FeS and M-FeS@C, is attributable to the formation of a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer on the electrode surface (Fig. S17a, b) [26,44,50]. Except for the first sodiation profile, subsequent profiles were almost the same with no capacity fading, indicating high reversibility.

To further analyze the electrochemical properties of the M-FeS@C+CNT, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 for the initial five cycles (Fig. 4d). The sodium-ion storage performance of the M-FeS has not been previously reported, and the sodium-ion storage kinetics of other iron sulfide phases are similar. Therefore, the CV curves were analyzed considering other phases of iron sulfide. During the first sodiation process (cathodic scan), several reduction peaks occurred. These peaks are likely related to the intercalation of sodium ions into the 2D M-FeS layers [51,84], further sodiation to form a Na x FeS phase (Na-rich phase) and an SEI layer [51,85], and a conversion reaction to form Fe and Na2S [86,87]. During the first desodiation (anodic scan), a broad peak near 1.35 V and sharp peaks near 1.57 and 1.95 V appeared, which could be attributable to diverse oxidation stages to form the Na x FeS phase and further deintercalation of sodium ions to recover the FeS phase [51,85–87]. The subsequent CV curves show good repetition, indicating reversible redox reactions of the M-FeS@C+CNT anode for sodium-ion storage. These characteristic peaks observed in CV curves are consistent with peaks in dQ/dV curves (Fig. S18).

The effect of introducing CNTs (carbon shell and CNT conductive agent) is more clearly shown in the cycle stability test at 1 A g−1 after two cycles of the activation process (Fig. 4e). Whereas M-FeS showed rapid capacity decay after the 200 cycles, M-FeS@C and M-FeS@C+CNT exhibited more stable and highly reversible cycling stability for 500 and 800 cycles, respectively, before cell failure; After 500 cycles, the M-FeS@C delivered a reversible capacity of 372 mAh g−1 with 99.0 % Coulombic efficiency and 85.7 % capacity retention. After 860 cycles, the M-FeS@C+CNT exhibited a reversible capacity of 366 mAh g−1 with 98.9 % Coulombic efficiency and an 89.3 % capacity retention. These results indicate that the M-FeS@C and M-FeS@C+CNT electrodes showed little structural change during cycling, attributable to the carbon shell exerting a clamping effect that increases the mechanical stability of the electrode. In particular, the improvement in the structural stability of the M-FeS@C+CNT compared with that of the M-FeS@C indicates that both effects—carbon shell and 3D conductive network—are synergic factors that maintain the mechanical stability of the electrode. When only the 3D conductive network was formed without forming a carbon shell (M-FeS+CNT), the rate capability was similar to that of M-FeS@C, but the cycle stability was noticeably inferior to the material with the carbon shell (Fig. S19). Notably, the cycle stability of M-FeS was improved by the simple addition of the Ox-SWCNTs conductive agent, implying that Ox-SWCNT itself can enhance the mechanical stability of the electrode [83]. This trend was also observed in comparing the cycle stability of the M-FeS@C and M-FeS@C+CNT samples. These results suggest that the formation of a carbon shell should precede the addition of a CNT conductive agent for synergic effects to achieve a high rate capability and highly stable cycle stability.

Moreover, outstanding improvements were achieved using only a small quantity (1 wt%) of Ox-SWCNTs (Fig. S20), a one-step low-temperature hydrothermal synthesis process, and 5 wt% of Ox-SWCNT conductive agents. Notably, the effect was realized with M-FeS with micron-sized particles, not nano-size which can accommodate stress and strain better during the charge–discharge process [27,75]. Compared with other methods that use a nano-sized active material and a large amount of carbon source, have a complicated synthesis procedure, and require high temperatures for hybridization with the carbon material, our strategy is highly facile and efficient. In addition, the hybrid structure synthesized using the proposed strategy results in comparable rate capability and cycle stability with those of previously reported strategies (Table S2).

The mechanical stability of the electrode was confirmed by ex-situ SEM observations (Fig. S21). After 1 cycle, all of the electrodes exhibited a plate-type particle morphology similar to that of a pristine electrode. However, after 20 cycles, although M-FeS@C and M-FeS@C+CNT electrodes maintained particle shape identical to that of the pristine electrodes, the M-FeS electrode exhibited an indiscernible particle morphology. This improved structural stability of M-FeS@C and M-FeS@C+CNT electrodes affected cycle stability, reaffirming the importance of the carbon shell and 3D conductive network for structure stabilization. In particular, the role of the carbon shell should be emphasized. First, the volume expansion during sodiation has been reported to be ∼ 170 % [26], which is small compared with the ∼ 400 % volume change caused by the alloying reaction between silicon and lithium ions [88,89]. Consistent with the ex situ SEM results (Fig. S21), our carbon shell is sufficiently robust to withstand the volume change during the charge–discharge process. Second, in addition to the effect of withstanding the volume change, the carbon shell appears to act like a container that limits the migration of conversion products, thereby restricting the shuttling effect [50,90,91]. SEM coupled with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM/EDS) analysis was conducted to determine the Fe/S atomic ratio (Table S3). After 20 cycles, the Fe/S atomic ratio increased in M-FeS, whereas the ratio remained similar to that in the pristine electrode in the case of M-FeS@C and M-FeS@C+CNT. These results indicate that the carbon shell can effectively restrict the shuttling of polysulfide, thereby preventing the loss of the iron sulfide anode material and consequently assisting the reversible conversion reaction without gradual capacity fade. These effects resulting from the formation of carbon shells improve the structural and electrochemical stability simultaneously. Structural changes during the charge–discharge process also affect the impedance (Fig. S22). Before cycling, all of the samples exhibited similar charge transfer resistances (R ct). After 20 cycles, the R ct values in M-FeS@C and M-FeS@C+CNT were smaller than that of M-FeS. These results suggest the important role of the carbon shell and CNT conductive agent in increasing the stability of the electrode and securing a conducting path, thereby resulting in a superior rate capability and cycle stability.

To understand the charge storage mechanism of the M-FeS@C+CNT, CV test at various scan rates from 0.1 to 1 mV s−1 was performed (Fig. 6 a). The charge storage contribution was analyzed on the basis of the b values calculated according to the following equations [85,86]:
(1)
i = a
v
b
( 0.5 ≤ b ≤ 1 )
(2)
log i = b log v + log a
where a and b are adjustable constants, v is the scan rate, and i is the peak current. The b-value is the slope determined from a plot of log i vs log v. A b value close to 1 indicates that the capacitive-controlled process is dominant for sodium-ion storage. In contrast, b-value close to 0.5 indicates that the diffusion-controlled process is dominant for sodium-ion storage. The b-values of the M-FeS@C+CNT were close to 0.5 for peak 6, and the others were close to 1, indicating that the capacitive-controlled process dominates the sodium-ion storage behavior (Fig. 6b). The capacitive contribution can be further evaluated by the determining the k 1 value according to the following equations [85,86]:
(3)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
(4)
i /
v
1 / 2
=
k 1
v
1 / 2
+
k 2
where k 1 v represents the capacitive-controlled contribution (non-faradaic) and k 2 v 1/2 is the diffusion-controlled contribution (faradaic). The k 1 value is the slope determined from a plot of the i/v 1/2 vs v 1/2. The results show that M-FeS@C+CNT exhibited a high ratio of capacitive-controlled portion, especially the highest portion of 85.2 % at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1 (Fig. 6c). With an increase in the scan rate, the capacitive-controlled contribution increased, consistent with the superior rate capability and cycle performance of the M-FeS@C+CNT electrode (Fig. 6d). The high electrochemical performance of M-FeS@C+CNT is ascribed to the fact that the introduction of a 3D conductive network leads to rapid electron transfer within the entire electrode and that the carbon shell helps maintain a stable structure.

We developed a novel hybridization strategy to construct a uniform carbon shell on the surface of micron-sized mackinawite iron sulfide by controlling the length of Ox-SWCNTs and using a one-step hydrothermal method under low-temperature (125 °C). The hybrid structure formed by nucleation and growth of M-FeS on carbon dots with functional groups exhibited improved the rate capabilities and cycle stabilities over 500 cycles. Even when Ox-SWCNTs were used as a conductive agent, rate capabilities and cycle performances were dramatically improved, reaching a high capacity of 332 mAh g−1 at a current density of even 10 A g−1 and a capacity of 360 mAh g−1 for 860 cycles at 1 A g−1, respectively. These remarkably improved rate capabilities and cycle stability were attributed to the synergic effect; the carbon shell formed on the surface of the M-FeS and the Ox-SWCNTs conductive agent that connect active materials, thereby mitigating volume changes and preventing degradation of the electrodes.
